claim_chain: We investigated how investments to scale-up of HIV, TB, and malaria services have influenced PHC in 108 LMICs
We estimated substantial reductions in PHC utilization among these persons, with 6.9 billion outpatient visits and 3.9 billion inpatient bed-days averted over the year 2000–2023
These reductions have freed up a median of 2.7% hospital beds across the 108 countries, 18.0% across countries in the SSA region
The total averted PHC utilization translated into US$135 billion in averted costs
This work adds to the body of literature documenting the complex consequences of global health initiatives to address specific diseases in resource-limited settings

Our findings underscore the positive contribution that sustained support by international donors and domestic governments for HIV, TB, and malaria elimination can make to the broader agenda of promoting health system resilience and efficiency

typical_pattern: Opening statement of what was investigated or estimated
Presentation of quantitative findings with specific numbers and uncertainty intervals
Contextualization of results by region or income level
Connection to broader literature or policy implications
Acknowledgment of limitations or alternative interpretations
Concluding statement about significance or future directions

likely_priorities: Robustness of modeling assumptions and counterfactual scenarios
Quality and availability of input data, particularly healthcare utilization rates for untreated conditions
Generalizability of findings across diverse country contexts
Consideration of supply-side factors and health system capacity constraints
Treatment of correlated access barriers across HTM and PHC services
likely_attacks: Limited empirical evidence on healthcare utilization rates for individuals with untreated HIV, TB, or malaria, increasing uncertainty
Several analytic inputs only available as global averages or translated using regression adjustments, limiting country-specific interpretation
Assumption that PHC access for individuals without HIV, TB, and malaria would be the same across scenarios may not hold
Did not investigate implications of correlated patterns of disadvantage where population groups face systemic access barriers to both HTM and PHC services
Did not quantify changes in PHC service quality, timeliness, and patient satisfaction beyond utilization
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly lists limitations including: 'there is limited evidence on healthcare utilization rates for individuals with untreated HIV, TB, or malaria, increasing the uncertainty of our estimates'; 'several analytic inputs were only available as global averages or were translated to different country settings using regression adjustments. For this reason, outcomes for individual countries should be interpreted with caution'; 'our analysis assumed that PHC access for individuals without HIV, TB, and malaria would be the same across scenarios'; 'Some population groups can face systemic access barriers, limiting utilization of both HTM and PHC services. We did not investigate the implications of these correlated patterns of disadvantage'; and 'our analysis did not consider changes in PHC services beyond utilization. Changes in utilization could also affect service quality, timeliness, and patient satisfaction, but these outcomes were not considered in this study.'

examples.quoted_phrase: We estimated substantial reductions in PHC utilization among these persons
These reductions were greatest in sub-Saharan Africa and East Asia and Pacific regions
It is possible that empirical approaches, leveraging observed variation in HTM service coverage, could also be used to investigate the impact
outcomes for individual countries should be interpreted with caution
Our findings underscore the positive contribution that sustained support by international donors and domestic governments for HIV, TB, and malaria elimination can make to the broader agenda

phrase_evidence.phrase: We investigated how investments to scale-up of HIV, TB, and malaria services have influenced PHC
We estimated substantial reductions in PHC utilization
6.9 billion outpatient visits and 3.9 billion inpatient bed-days averted
as compared to a counterfactual scenario constraining HTM service coverage at year 2000 levels
These reductions have freed up a median of 2.7% hospital beds
representing US$135 billion in averted costs
This work adds to the body of literature documenting the complex consequences
demonstrate another mechanism through which these investments can contribute to health system strengthening
not usually quantified in other analyses
Under the constant coverage counterfactual scenario
low HTM service coverage allowed for continued disease evolution and spread
would seek primary care when symptoms emerged
The projected surge in demand for health services under the counterfactual scenarios illustrates the impact
progressive growth in the magnitude of averted PHC utilization and costs over the study period
as HTM services were scaled up and the compounding benefits of transmission reductions were realized
An exception to this pattern was malaria
This finding reveals how interruptions in intervention access can have secondary consequences
In sensitivity analyses, we considered three alternative analytic specifications
it is notable that estimated averted costs were substantial under each of the specifications we explored
This study has several limitations
there is limited evidence on healthcare utilization rates for individuals with untreated HIV, TB, or malaria, increasing the uncertainty of our estimates
outcomes for individual countries should be interpreted with caution
It is possible that empirical approaches, leveraging observed variation in HTM service coverage, could also be used
further research is needed to illuminate the societal and economic impacts
This analysis provides evidence on how investments to scale up HIV, TB, and malaria services over the start of the 21st century have reduced the burden
Our findings underscore the positive contribution that sustained support by international donors and domestic governments for HIV, TB, and malaria elimination can make
While it is unclear how these costs would have been financed
they would have put increasing strain on health systems already experiencing capacity and funding constraints
Results shown for 10 modeled countries with the highest HIV prevalence, TB incidence rate, and malaria incidence rate in 2023
Countries where averted inpatient utilization was over 50% of national hospital bed capacity

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. Quantitative values match those in the paper. No external references, author names, or invented data were included.

_phrase_violations.phrase: representing US$135 billion in averted costs
